questions.question: What is the STaR loop, in one pass?
options: Distill a large model into a smaller one
Sample a rationale + answer, keep the trace if the answer is correct, fine-tune on kept traces, repeat
Train on human-written chains of thought only
Generate answers without rationales and grade them later
questions.explanation: STaR (Zelikman et al., 2022) bootstraps reasoning from the model's own rationales, graded by final-answer correctness — improving GSM8K and CommonsenseQA without new human annotation. It is the minimum-viable self-improvement loop.

questions.question: Why does STaR need "rationalization" for problems the model always gets wrong?
options: To make the dataset larger for its own sake
The keep-if-correct filter can never collect training data on those problems — so STaR injects the correct answer as a hint and keeps the rationale written toward it
To punish the model for failures
Because fine-tuning requires balanced classes
questions.explanation: Without rationalization the loop only learns where the model already sometimes succeeds. Hinted rationales unlock the rest — at the cost that rationalized reasoning may be implausible, a known failure mode.

questions.question: What does V-STaR do with the incorrect rationales STaR throws away?
options: Deletes them more aggressively
Uses both correct and incorrect solutions to train a DPO-based verifier that ranks sampled rationales at inference time
Feeds them to a larger teacher model
Publishes them as a negatives dataset
questions.explanation: Every (rationale, correct?) pair is verifier training data. Best-of-N selection with the verifier delivered +4 to +17 points over self-improvement baselines on GSM8K and MATH — most of the gain from inference-time selection.

questions.question: What safety concern do STaR, V-STaR, and Quiet-STaR all share?
options: They require too much compute to audit
The gradient signal is the final answer — shortcut reasoning that happens to land right gets reinforced, working in-distribution and breaking silently out-of-distribution
They leak training data verbatim
They make models overly cautious
questions.explanation: Answer-conditioned training preserves any shortcut that reached the right answer. Even V-STaR's verifier is trained on the same labels and can prefer well-formatted wrong reasoning. Mitigations: process-supervised rewards and held-out OOD evals that break shortcuts.

questions.question: How does the STaR pattern map onto the 2025-2026 frontier stack?
options: It doesn't — STaR is obsolete
RL on verifiable problems (DeepSeek-R1, o1) is the same answer-conditioned signal scaled up; AlphaEvolve is STaR for code with an evaluator; DGM is STaR for the agent scaffold itself
STaR became the transformer architecture
Only Quiet-STaR is still used, renamed to RLHF
questions.explanation: The keep-what-worked loop reappears everywhere: verifiable-reward RL, process reward models as the supervised alternative, and the evolutionary systems of the next two lessons. Understanding STaR makes all of them click.
